Article.ArticleTitle: The role of silicomanganese slag in blast furnace slag-steel slag-FGD gypsum-based clinker-free cementitious materials: compressive strength, hydration mechanisms, and Mn solidification behavior.

Abstract.AbstractText: Silicomanganese slag (SiMnS) is a by-product of silicon manganese alloy smelting. Due to its complex composition and potential environmental risks, there are significant challenges in its resource utilization. Based on this, this study developed an SiMnS-blast furnace slag (BFS)-steel slag (SS)-flue gas desulfurization (FGD) gypsum-based clinker-free cementitious material (SSGCM) and systematically investigated the mechanical properties, hydration reaction mechanism, and manganese solidification behavior of SSGCM. The results indicate that the SS content and FGD gypsum content are the main factors affecting the compressive strength at 3 days/7 days and 28 days, respectively. The optimal mix ratio is L8 (SS content, FGD gypsum content, and SiMnS/BFS ratio of 23%, 14%, and 1.8/3.0, respectively), with a compressive strength of 47.09 MPa at 28 days. The hydration mechanism indicates that the active [AlO4] in SiMnS/BFS continuously participates in hydration from 3 days to 56 days, forming highly crystalline AFt/AFm. Meanwhile, the extensive dissolution of active [AlO4] induced an increase in the structural defects of the silica-alumina glass in SiMnS/BFS, which in turn drove the depolymerization and dissolution of [SiO4], promoting an increase in the mean chain length (MCL) of the C-(A)-S-H gel. This was reflected in the relative content of Q2 (1Al) and Q2p in the C-(A)-S-H gel, which increased to 9.17% and 10.28%, respectively. The manganese solidification test shows that the solidification rate of manganese in the SSGCM after 56 days exceeds 99.2%. C-(A)-S-H gel has an encapsulating effect on manganese, causing the acid-extractable fraction of manganese to gradually transform into the residual fraction. This study provides a theoretical basis for the development of solid waste-based cementitious materials and the resource utilization of SiMnS.
